name: Ce La Vi
description: Rooftop restaurant and sky bar perched on level 57 of Marina Bay Sands. Stunning 360° views of the Singapore skyline. Come for sunset cocktails or the tasting menu.
address: 1 Bayfront Ave, Tower 3 Level 57, Marina Bay Sands, Singapore 018971

name: Wild Honey
description: All-day breakfast institution on Orchard Road serving beautifully plated dishes inspired by morning meals from around the world — think Tunisian shakshuka, Scandinavian smoked salmon, and an Australian full. Always busy; arrive early or expect a wait.

name: Atlas Bar
description: Grand art deco gin palace inside a 1920s-style lobby tower. One of the world's great bar rooms — 1,300 gins on a three-storey tower, excellent cocktails, and an atmosphere that rewards lingering. Perfect for a long afternoon.
address: 600 North Bridge Rd, Parkview Square, Singapore 188778

name: Long Bar at Raffles
description: The birthplace of the Singapore Sling. Colonial rattan fans, peanut shells on the floor, and that drink. Touristy? Yes. Worth it? Also yes. Order the original recipe and sit in the main hall.

name: Jumbo Seafood
description: The definitive chilli crab experience at Clarke Quay. Order the black pepper crab too. Messy, loud, and completely essential. Book ahead — the waterfront terrace fills up fast.

name: Lau Pa Sat
description: Victorian cast-iron hawker centre in the heart of the financial district. Best at lunch when office workers pack every table. Satay stalls fire up at night along the adjacent street. A genuine Singapore institution.

name: Clarke Quay
description: Singapore's riverside entertainment strip — restored shophouses crammed with bars, restaurants, and clubs along the Singapore River. Best explored on foot in the early evening before it gets crowded. Good starting point for a night out.

name: Haji Lane
description: A narrow Kampong Glam alley lined with indie boutiques, vintage shops, and colourful street murals. Excellent for a slow afternoon browse followed by drinks at one of the lane's small bars. The surrounding Arab Quarter is equally worth exploring.

name: The Biosphere
description: Gardens by the Bay's two climate-controlled conservatories — the Flower Dome (world's largest glass greenhouse) and the Cloud Forest (a 35-metre indoor waterfall surrounded by rare mountain plants). A genuine engineering marvel in the middle of the city.

name: Marina Bay Sands
description: The iconic three-tower hotel connected by a rooftop infinity pool. Home to Ce La Vi, a casino, and the Shoppes mall. The SkyPark observation deck offers the best panoramic view of Marina Bay. The visual centrepiece of modern Singapore.

name: Sentosa Island
description: Singapore's resort island connected to the mainland by cable car, monorail, and boardwalk. Home to Universal Studios, beach clubs, and the southern tip of continental Asia.

name: Lazarus Island Beach
description: One of Singapore's most pristine and uncrowded beaches, accessible only by ferry from Marina South Pier. Crystal clear water, white sand, and almost no development. Bring your own supplies.

name: National Gallery Singapore
description: The region's largest museum of modern Singapore and Southeast Asian art, housed inside two national monuments — the former Supreme Court and City Hall. Free to enter the main atrium. The rooftop bar Smoke & Mirrors has one of the best views of the Padang.

name: Sultan Mosque
description: The focal point of Singapore's Muslim community and the centrepiece of Kampong Glam — an imposing 1928 mosque with a golden dome visible from several streets away. Visitors are welcome outside prayer times. The surrounding Arab Quarter is excellent for exploring on foot.

name: Sri Mariamman Temple
description: The oldest Hindu temple in Singapore, built in 1827 in the heart of Chinatown. The ornate gopuram tower above the entrance is covered in hundreds of painted figures from Hindu mythology — one of the most visually striking facades in the city. Free entry.

name: Armenian Church
description: The oldest Christian church in Singapore — a neoclassical 1835 building by George Coleman in a quiet garden near the Civic District. Small, perfectly proportioned, and completely off the tourist circuit despite being a national monument. Free entry.

name: CHIJMES
description: A 19th-century Gothic chapel and convent complex transformed into one of Singapore's most atmospheric dining and drinking precincts — restaurants and bars set around a beautifully lit courtyard. The chapel itself hosts concerts and events. Best in the evening.

name: St Andrew's Cathedral
description: Singapore's largest cathedral and a stellar example of colonial Gothic Revival architecture — white plastered walls, flying buttresses, and a spire that has anchored the Civic District skyline since 1862. Free entry. A genuinely peaceful spot in the middle of the city.

name: Chinatown Food Street
description: Smith Street in Chinatown — a pedestrianised al fresco hawker street lined with open-air food stalls serving laksa, satay, chilli crab, and BBQ seafood. One of the most atmospheric places in Singapore to eat outside at night.

name: Chinatown Heritage Centre
description: Three restored shophouses in Pagoda Street recreating the cramped living conditions of Singapore's early Chinese immigrants — evocative dioramas, oral histories, and artefacts that bring the 19th-century migrant experience vividly to life.

name: Thian Hock Keng Temple
description: The oldest and most important Hokkien temple in Singapore, built in 1839 without a single nail and dedicated to Mazu, the Goddess of the Sea. Beautifully restored, with cast-iron railings from Glasgow and Dutch tiles alongside intricate Chinese craftsmanship.

name: Eurasian Heritage Centre
description: A heritage museum in the Katong area dedicated to Singapore's Eurasian community — one of the country's oldest and most distinctive communities, with roots in Portuguese, Dutch, and British colonial Singapore. A genuine insight into a culture rarely covered elsewhere.

name: Tiong Bahru Shophouses
description: Singapore's best-preserved Art Deco residential neighbourhood — streamline Moderne flats built in the 1930s now surrounded by excellent independent cafés, bookshops, and bakeries. Walk Seng Poh Road and the surrounding streets for the full effect.
address: Seng Poh Road, Tiong Bahru, Singapore

name: Capitol Singapore
description: A restored 1930s entertainment complex near City Hall — the Capitol Theatre's original facade preserved alongside a hotel and retail block. The building's Palladian exterior is one of the finest examples of colonial-era architecture in Singapore.

name: Esplanade Theatres on the Bay
description: Singapore's premier performing arts centre — nicknamed 'the Durian' for its spiky aluminium sunshade shell. The outdoor concourse stages free performances most evenings. Check the calendar for what's on — the programming ranges from Singaporean theatre to international orchestras.

name: The Interlace
description: OMA's landmark residential complex in the southern part of the island — 31 apartment blocks stacked in a hexagonal arrangement like a vast game of Jenga. One of the most significant works of contemporary architecture in Singapore. Best seen from Depot Road.

name: PARKROYAL on Pickering
description: WOHA's biophilic hotel in Chinatown — a hotel building that appears to grow out of a terraced garden, with 15,000 square metres of sky gardens, waterfalls, and planted terraces cascading down the facade. One of the most photographed buildings in Singapore.

name: LASALLE College of the Arts
description: A dramatic 2007 campus by RSP Architects — interconnected glass pavilions arranged around an open courtyard that evokes a rain forest canopy. The Institute of Contemporary Arts inside hosts free exhibitions. Worth visiting even if you have no interest in the college.

name: Kranji War Memorial
description: A hilltop cemetery in the north of Singapore honouring over 4,400 Commonwealth servicemen and women killed during the Malayan campaign and the fall of Singapore in World War II. Quiet, beautifully maintained, and genuinely moving.

name: Sri Veeramakaliamman Temple
description: One of the oldest and most visually striking Hindu temples in Singapore, in the heart of Little India — the elaborate gopuram is covered in colourful sculptures of deities. Particularly spectacular during the Deepavali festival in October.

name: Maghain Aboth Synagogue
description: The oldest synagogue in Southeast Asia, built in 1878 — a Moorish-influenced building on Waterloo Street that serves Singapore's small Jewish community. Visits by appointment only, but the exterior alone is worth seeing from the street.

name: Malay Heritage Centre
description: A museum dedicated to the history and culture of Singapore's Malay community, housed in the former Istana Kampong Gelam — the palace of the Sultans of Singapore. The kampong and the surrounding Kampong Glam neighbourhood are equally worth exploring.

name: Peranakan Museum
description: Singapore's finest museum dedicated to Peranakan (Straits Chinese) culture — exquisite porcelain, embroidered clothing, jewellery, and furniture in a beautifully converted 1910 schoolhouse. One of the best small museums in Southeast Asia.

name: Raffles Hotel
description: Singapore's most iconic colonial hotel, open since 1887 and fully restored in 2019 — grand whitewashed Baroque architecture, the Long Bar where the Singapore Sling was invented, and a calm courtyard garden. Worth walking through even if you're not staying.

name: Gillman Barracks
description: A former British military barracks converted into Singapore's contemporary art gallery district — around a dozen international galleries in colonial bungalows set in a forested compound. Free entry to all galleries. One of the most peaceful places in the city.

name: ArtScience Museum
description: Moshe Safdie's lotus-shaped museum at Marina Bay Sands — the ten 'petals' house world-class touring exhibitions at the intersection of art, science, culture, and technology. One of Singapore's most distinctive buildings from both inside and out.

name: Singapore Art Museum
description: Singapore's contemporary art museum focused on Southeast Asian art — housed in a restored 1855 Catholic school building on Bras Basah Road. The permanent collection spans painting, sculpture, and moving image across the region.

name: National Design Centre
description: Singapore's hub for design culture — exhibitions, talks, and a design library in a restored 1845 convent building on Middle Road. The exhibitions change regularly and the building itself is worth seeing.

name: Victoria Theatre & Concert Hall
description: Singapore's oldest performing arts venue — a Victorian Renaissance building on the waterfront that reopened in 2014 after a four-year restoration. Home to the Singapore Symphony Orchestra. The clock tower is one of the most photographed colonial landmarks in the city.

name: Asian Civilisations Museum
description: Eleven galleries of Asian art and artefacts in the 1865 Empress Place Building beside the Singapore River — ancient Chinese ceramics, Southeast Asian bronzes, South Asian textiles, and West Asian metalwork. One of Singapore's best museums.

name: National Museum of Singapore
description: Singapore's oldest museum in a magnificent 1887 colonial building with a glass rotunda addition — progressive galleries telling the country's history from Sang Nila Utama through independence. The stained-glass dome alone is worth visiting.

name: MINT Museum of Toys
description: Five floors of vintage toys and memorabilia from over 40 countries spanning the 1840s to the 1980s — over 50,000 items including tin toys, action figures, and automata. A genuinely unexpected delight near Raffles Hotel.

name: Sun Yat Sen Memorial Hall
description: A white Edwardian villa in Novena that served as the Southeast Asian operational base of Dr Sun Yat Sen's revolutionary movement to overthrow the Qing Dynasty. A carefully curated museum of Chinese revolutionary history in an unlikely setting.

name: Changi Museum
description: An emotional exploration of Singapore during the Japanese Occupation — recreated prison cells, personal testimonies, and the famous Changi Murals. Located near the original Changi Prison site in the east of the island. Essential context for understanding Singapore's history.

name: Old Ford Factory
description: The site where the British surrendered to Japan on 15 February 1942 — the former Ford assembly plant in Bukit Timah now houses a museum documenting the fall of Singapore and the Japanese Occupation. A significant and sobering piece of history.

name: Singapore City Gallery
description: The Urban Redevelopment Authority's free exhibition space showing the physical transformation of Singapore through scale models, interactive displays, and urban planning documents. The centrepiece 1:400 scale model of the entire city is extraordinary.

name: Merlion Park
description: The waterfront park housing Singapore's most famous symbol — the half-lion, half-fish Merlion statue at the mouth of the Singapore River. Touristy but the view of Marina Bay Sands from here is genuinely one of the great urban panoramas in Asia.

name: Singapore Flyer
description: One of the world's largest observation wheels at 165 metres — 30-minute capsule rides with views stretching to Batam, Indonesia and the Johor Bahru skyline on a clear day. Best at dusk when the city lights up across the bay.

name: Marina Barrage
description: A dam across the Marina Channel that created Singapore's first city reservoir — the rooftop green provides one of the best unobstructed views of the Marina Bay skyline and is a popular kite-flying spot on weekends. Free to visit.

name: Henderson Waves
description: Singapore's highest pedestrian bridge — a sinuous wave-shaped walkway 36 metres above Henderson Road, connecting Telok Blangah Hill Park to Mount Faber as part of the Southern Ridges trail. Particularly beautiful at night when the structure is lit.

name: Pinnacle@Duxton
description: The world's tallest public housing development — seven 50-storey towers connected by sky gardens at levels 26 and 50. The 50th-floor skybridge is open to visitors for a small fee and offers a panoramic view of Tanjong Pagar and the southern port.

name: Hajjah Fatimah Mosque
description: A 19th-century mosque in Kampong Glam notable for its slightly tilting minaret — built in a hybrid style blending European, Malay, and Chinese architectural elements. One of Singapore's six national monument mosques.

name: Haw Par Villa
description: A 1937 theme park of Chinese mythology on Pasir Panjang Road — thousands of statues and dioramas depicting scenes from Chinese folklore, legends, and moral tales. The infamous Ten Courts of Hell exhibit is not for the faint-hearted. Free entry and genuinely unforgettable.

name: NUS Baba House
description: A meticulously restored 1895 Peranakan townhouse in Neil Road preserving the original family's possessions, furniture, and decorative items in situ — a rare example of a 19th-century Singapore home frozen in time. Visits by guided tour only.

name: Hong San See Temple
description: A 1908 Hokkien temple on Mohamed Sultan Road, restored by craftsmen from Fujian province to its original condition — intricate wood carvings, painted ceramic figures on the ridge, and a remarkable painted ceiling. A national monument that receives very few visitors.

name: Kampong Glam
description: Singapore's historic Malay and Arab quarter — the Sultan Mosque, the Istana Kampong Gelam palace, Haji Lane's indie boutiques, and Arab Street's textile and carpet shops. One of the most atmospheric and walkable neighbourhoods in Singapore.

name: Red Dot Design Museum
description: A museum in the former Traffic Police building holding over 1,000 Red Dot Award-winning products — from furniture and consumer electronics to packaging and visual communication. The building itself, a Modernist 1930s structure, is worth seeing.

name: Reflections at Bukit Chandu
description: A heritage centre in a restored colonial bungalow on Pasir Panjang Ridge commemorating the Battle of Pasir Panjang — one of the fiercest engagements of the Malayan campaign in February 1942. The surrounding Kent Ridge Park is worth exploring.

name: Kampong Glam & Bras Basah Arts District
description: The cultural precinct stretching from the Civic District to Kampong Glam — museums, heritage buildings, art schools, and the Bugis street market. Singapore's most concentrated area of cultural institutions within easy walking distance of each other.

name: Fort Canning Park
description: A hilltop park in the heart of the city with 700 years of history — from the seat of Malay royalty to a British fort to the Allied command centre during WWII. The WWII Battle Box bunker and the spice garden are both worth visiting.

name: East Coast Park
description: A 15km coastal park along the eastern shoreline — Singapore's most popular outdoor space for cycling, rollerblading, kite flying, and eating seafood at open-air restaurants along the beach. The lagoon end has decent swimming. Rent bikes at the park entrances.

name: Singapore Botanic Gardens
description: A UNESCO World Heritage Site and the green heart of Singapore — 183 acres of themed gardens, heritage trees, and the National Orchid Garden with 1,000 species and 2,000 hybrids. Free to enter the main gardens. One of the great tropical gardens in the world.

name: MacRitchie Nature Reserve
description: Singapore's oldest nature reserve surrounding MacRitchie Reservoir — walking trails through primary rainforest, a 250-metre TreeTop Walk suspension bridge, and wild macaques and monitor lizards throughout. The full loop trail takes about 3 hours.

name: Singapore Zoo & Night Safari
description: The best open-concept zoo in Asia — animals in naturalistic habitats across themed zones in the Mandai forest. The adjacent Night Safari is the world's first nocturnal wildlife park. Book the tram ride for the Night Safari.

name: Pulau Ubin
description: A small island off the northeast coast accessible by bumboat from Changi Village — one of the last places in Singapore that looks like it did in the 1960s. Rent a bicycle, explore the kampong, and visit Chek Jawa's mangrove wetlands at the eastern tip.

name: Little India
description: Singapore's most sensory neighbourhood — flower garland stalls, spice shops, sari boutiques, and Tamil temples along Serangoon Road. Best on a Sunday evening when the community gathers. The Tekka Centre food court inside has excellent banana leaf rice.

name: Orchard Road
description: Asia's most famous shopping street — 2.2km of malls, flagship stores, and covered walkways. Ion Orchard, Paragon, and Ngee Ann City anchor the main stretch. The basement food courts are excellent and air-conditioned. Dazzlingly lit during Christmas.

name: Joo Chiat & Katong
description: Singapore's best-preserved Peranakan neighbourhood in the east — brightly painted shophouses, excellent laksa, and a slower pace than the city centre. East Coast Road and Joo Chiat Road are both worth walking. The stretch of terraces near 328 Katong Laksa is the highlight.

name: Chinatown
description: Singapore's historic Chinese quarter — restored shophouses, Pagoda Street's souvenir stalls, Sri Mariamman Temple, the Buddha Tooth Relic Temple, and some of the city's best hawker food. Best in the early morning or evening when the heat eases.

name: Buddha Tooth Relic Temple
description: A Tang Dynasty-inspired Buddhist temple in Chinatown housing a tooth relic of the Buddha — four floors of Buddhist art and artefacts culminating in a rooftop garden and 300kg gold stupa in the relic chamber. Free entry to all levels.

name: Southern Ridges Walk
description: A 10km trail connecting Mount Faber, Telok Blangah Hill, and Kent Ridge through forest canopy — the Henderson Waves bridge is the architectural centrepiece. Best walked in the early morning or at dusk. Takes about 3-4 hours end to end.

name: Mount Faber & Cable Car
description: A forested hill in the southern part of Singapore with harbour views and the cable car station linking to Sentosa — the cable car ride over the sea is one of the best views of the port. The hilltop restaurants are decent enough to justify a sunset dinner.

name: Kusu Island
description: A small island south of Singapore accessible by ferry from Marina South Pier — a Taoist temple, a Malay shrine, and a clean lagoon beach with calm water. Best visited on a weekday when the island is nearly empty. The pilgrimage season in October draws large crowds.

name: Chek Jawa Wetlands
description: One of Singapore's most biodiverse ecosystems on the eastern tip of Pulau Ubin — mangroves, coral rubble flats, and seagrass lagoons visible from a 1km coastal boardwalk. A 21-metre viewing tower overlooks the wetlands. Only accessible as part of a Pulau Ubin trip.

name: Labrador Nature Reserve
description: A coastal nature reserve in the southwest with WWI and WWII fortifications hidden in the jungle — tunnels, gun emplacements, and a sea wall. The most historically layered green space in Singapore, almost always quiet.

name: Sungei Buloh Wetland Reserve
description: Singapore's premier birdwatching reserve in the far north — migratory shorebirds from September to March, resident monitor lizards, otters, and mudskippers year-round. The boardwalk loops take 1-2 hours. Bring binoculars.

name: Universal Studios Singapore
description: Southeast Asia's only Universal Studios theme park on Sentosa — seven themed zones from Hollywood to Ancient Egypt to Jurassic World. The Battlestar Galactica duelling roller coasters and the Transformers ride are the headline attractions.

name: S.E.A. Aquarium
description: One of the world's largest aquariums on Sentosa — over 100,000 marine animals in 49 habitats, including the Open Ocean exhibit with manta rays, sharks, and a 36-metre viewing panel. Best visited on a weekday morning.

name: Singapore River
description: The historic artery of Singapore — walk from Merlion Park past Boat Quay's restaurants to Clarke Quay and Robertson Quay along the north bank. The bumboat quays and restored shophouses tell the full story of Singapore's trading past. Best in the early evening.

name: Fuk Tak Chi Museum
description: A restored 1820s Hakka-Cantonese temple in Telok Ayer Street, once the oldest Chinese temple in Singapore and now a small museum — the terracotta roof tiles, lacquered screens, and original temple furniture are all in place.

name: Katong Antique House
description: A private Peranakan family home on East Coast Road open as an informal museum — the owner Peter Wee conducts tours through rooms filled with heirlooms, antique furniture, and family photographs spanning five generations of Straits Chinese life.

name: Chinese Garden
description: A classical Chinese imperial garden in Jurong Lake — stone bridges, pagodas, and covered walkways set around the lake in the west of Singapore. The seven-storey pagoda offers good views. Quiet on weekdays and particularly beautiful in the early morning.

name: HarbourFront
description: Singapore's southern gateway — the VivoCity mall, the cable car terminus to Sentosa, and the cruise terminal where ships depart for Indonesia and Malaysia. The rooftop of VivoCity has a good view of Sentosa and the southern islands.
